versions. Log Tracer searches the logs to ﬁnd evidences of the vulnerability status. Finally, Result Generator compares the results from Vulnerable Version Finder with the Known Affected Software Conﬁgurations of the NVD, and outputs the justiﬁed affected versions and the accuracy of the NVD.
Dataset. This article mainly selects the following products,

including Linux kernel, Openssl, BusyBox, Wget and QEMU. The source websites of their patches are shown in Table II . Taking Linux kernel as an example, after the ﬁnal screening, there are 1,367 CVE ﬁles in our database, including many vul- nerability types such as buffer overﬂow, integer overﬂow, SQL injection, use-after-free and many others. We collected Linux kernel vulnerabilities released from January 2003 to September 2020 from the NVD, which resulted in 2,576 CVE -IDs in total. Among them, 1,367 were published in https://git.kernel.org, including 2,664 patches. The rest correspond to the secondary development versions based on the Linux kernel, and their patches might not exist or were scattered on various third-party websites, so these CVE-IDs are not within our consideration. In addition, we collected a total of 3,086 Linux kernel versions that make up its version tree, taking 585 G of data space.
We scan 1,367 CVE-IDs of Linux kernel against its 3,086

open-sourceversionstodetectallvulnerableversionsinthethree directions (R1, R2 and R3). For our dataset, we manually verify the reported boundary of vulnerable versions in the version tree foreachCVE-ID.NotethatfortheTPsofthereportedvulnerable version set, we can verify them by manual inspection. However, for FNs, since there are a great number of software versions(we do not regard the versions reported by NVD as the ground truth because it has many errors, as presented in the next subsection), we cannot directly report the FN rate or recall. Therefore, for R1 and R2, we sample 100 CVE-IDs for manual inspection to calculate the recall of our tool. As for R3, we determine the lower bound of the recall. The evaluation results of our tool are as follows:
R1. It represents the method of obtaining the last vulnerable

version of Linux kernel for a given CVE-ID. Manual veriﬁcation shows that the reported vulnerable versions has no false positive, and the precision reaches 100%. Among the 100 sampled CVE- IDs, there are unpatched codes in the next major versions with respect to 4 CVE-ids, and there is no repatch found. So the lower bound of the recall rate is 96%. The incomplete patches and errors in git logs are possible causes behind these false negative cases.
R2. It represents the method of determining the last vulnerable

version in each version branch. Our manual inspection shows that the precision of the reported versions also reaches 100%. We again sample 100 different CVE-IDs for manual inspection, which shows that there are unpatched codes in the next minor versions with respect to 2 CVE-IDs, so the recall rate is at least 98% for the samples. The reasons for the false negatives are as above.

version V from bybackwardtraversingthemajorversionsbefore the minimum committed version (reported by changelog) in the version tree. As shown in Table II , among the 1,367 CVE-IDs, 337 of them have some partial matching versions and the re- maining 1,030 only have exact matching versions. Among the matching results, we add up the number of vulnerable versions detected by each CVE-ID, and the total number is 37,685, including 32,903 exact matching versions and 4,782 partial matching versions. Because different CVE-IDs may correspond to different source code fragments SF s in the same version, a vulnerable version may be counted more than one time. For the 1,030 exact matching only CVE-IDs, since the ﬁrst exact matching version will be regarded as V from , we ﬁrst check the versions right before our reported version V from . Among them, it is found that either the CVE-speciﬁed ﬁle does not exist, or there is no vulnerable function ( Fun ) in the source code. Therefore, the V from under our exact matching method has no false negative. Our tool spent 10.2 hours for this process in total.
For the remaining 337 CVE-IDs with partial matches, we

only check the major versions with different partial matching code, not the minor versions. Our statistics shows that on average one CVE-ID corresponds to 2 different sf-diffs. To evaluate our partial matching algorithm and obtain the accurate V from for each CVE-ID, we need to check at least 532 times, and we spent about 3.3 hours to verity the partial matching code. Since the partial matching code is similar to the CVE patch, we can determine that the code has a potential vulnerability, but whether it has a real vulnerability or not needs to be conﬁrmed through context-based dynamic analysis and POC construction, which requires huge investment and cost. Fortunately, git logs record the ﬁx process of a vulnerability, including the release of CVE, which is the ground truth for our justiﬁcation of the vulnerability. However, if the git log information did not record whether the code is related to a vulnerability, it is probable that the code has a vulnerability, or it may be just a bug that was ﬁxed. Speciﬁ- cally, if a git log is in the following two situations, we cannot directly determine whether it has the vulnerability: (1) The log information is incomplete. (2) the log information is complete, but it does not record the relationship with the vulnerability. In this case, the V from for this CVE-ID is the earliest vulnerable version in the version tree that we can manually determine.
